coredata.dc:title: Revolutionizing Gram-negative bacteria detection: FLIM and multicolor imaging based selective interaction study using colistin passivated carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Pathak, Abhishek
Navaneeth, Punnakkal
Gupta, Monika
Pradeep, Aarathi
Nair, Bipin G.
Suneesh, Punathil Vasu
Elangovan, Ravikrishnan
Sundberg, Lotta-Riina
Marjomäki, Varpu
Babu, T.G. Satheesh
coredata.dc:description: The rising prevalence of antibiotic-resistant Gram-negative bacteria presents a major challenge for healthcare and requires the development of new and effective detection techniques. In this study, we present a novel and simple method for the selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria using multi-emissive colistin-passivated carbon dots (m-CCD) based on Fluorescence Lifetime (FLT) response and bioimaging. The m-CCD was synthesized using a one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal process, and its interaction with Gram-negative bacteria was studied using Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) and automated Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM). The investigations revealed a notable reduction in the FLT of m-CCD-treated Gram-negative bacteria (average lifetime of m-CCD, (τ avg ) m-CCD is 3.91 ns and m-CCD treated Gram-negative bacteria, (τ avg ) m-CCD-bac is 1.57 ns), demonstrating the selective interaction between them. Furthermore, the CLSM data revealed high specificity of m-CCD for Gram-negative bacteria, even in polymicrobial samples. Importantly, we also demonstrate that this method can quantify Escherichia coli (E. coli) cells with two linear ranges such as 3.40 × 105 – 9.80 × 105 CFU mL−1 and 6.90 × 107–4.14 × 108 CFU mL−1 in standard culture and real samples such as tap water and human urine with a limit of detection ∼ 3.68–4.89 × 104 CFU mL−1. This straightforward method has great potential for the rapid and selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria, paving the way for early diagnosis and treatment of bacterial infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colistin
Carbon dots
Gram-negative bacteria
Bio-imaging
FLIM

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of colistin derived fluorescent m-CCD
Sample preparation of m-CCD treated bacteria for spectroscopic and microscopic analysis
FLIM based FLT measurement of m-CCD treated bacteria
Quantification of m-CCD treated E. coli cells
Specificity of m-CCD towards E. coli over S. aureus cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. The authors thank Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), CUSAT, Kerala, for their generous help in HR-TEM imaging of m-CCD. The authors also thank the Indian Institute of Technology-Kanpur for performing the XPS characterization of m-CCD. The authors are grateful to Sophisticated Analytical & Technical Help Institutes (SATHI) facilities situated at IIT-Delhi Sonipat campus for allowing us to carry out the advanced confocal and fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopic analysis.

The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. The authors thank Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), CUSAT, Kerala, for their generous help in HR-TEM imaging of m-CCD. The authors also thank the Indian Institute of Technology-Kanpur for performing the XPS characterization of m-CCD. The authors are grateful to Sophisticated Analytical & Technical Help Institutes (SATHI) facilities situated at IIT-Delhi Sonipat campus for allowing us to carry out the advanced confocal and fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopic analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) HR-TEM micrograph of m-CCD exhibiting the uniform spherical particles (inset a shows the magnified view of the spherical m-CCD) and the average diameter was measured as 4.6 ± 0.9 nm (inset b displays the particle size histogram plot of m-CCD); B) XRD pattern of m-CCD; C) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of m-CCD (black) and PL emission curve (red) (λex/λem ∼ 350/450 nm); inset shows the m-CCD solution in the bright day light (transparent) & UV light (blue color); D) PL emission scan at various excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 500 nm (inset shows scan from 430 nm to 500 nm); E) Fluorescence lifetime decay measurement of m-CCD at 325 nm excitation with fitting curve and residue with respect to time (inset) * Deconvolution model fit calculated using subtraction of FWHM of IRF from full decay curve & F) FTIR spectra of colistin and m-CCD.

A) FLIM based point lifetime decay curves of individual m-CCD treated E. coli measured on the cell wall (1, 2, 3 & 4) and cytoplasm (5) with IRF at 405 nm excitation (Inset: lifetime decay of free m-CCD in solution); FLIM based point lifetime decay at different ROI (triplicates) for data reproducibility B) ROI-1; C) ROI-2 D) ROI-3.

PL emission spectra of different concentrations of m-CCD treated E. coli in A) standard bacterial culture, B) tap water and C) urine sample measured at 350 nm excitation wavelength (inset: two linear regression plots with standard error bar of PL emission intensity vs lower and higher E. coli concentrations) D) PCA score plot for the m-CCD sensing system to differentiate Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria (4 m-CCD treated bacteria × triplicates × 4 cell concentrations, 108 to 105 CFU mL−1). Error bars account for three independent measurements and denoted the deviation present within the triplicate data.

A) and C) are PL emission responses of m-CCD treated E. coli cells and S. aureus cells with varying OD600 values from 0.1 to 1.0 at 350 nm excitation wavelength. B) and D) are the concentration of E. coli and S. aureus at OD600 vs. PL intensity curves with standard error bars, E) PL emission spectrum of m-CCD treated E. coli and S. aureus cells at 350 nm excitation wavelength, F) Histogram (with standard error bars) of PL emission response of untreated (C1-C4) and m-CCD treated Gram-negative (E. coli and P. aeruginosa) and Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and B. subtilis). Error bars reckoned from three repetitive experiments and indicated the deviation within the triplicate data.

(A) CLSM images of m-CCD treated E. coli, and S. aureus (0.1 mg mL−1) under 405 nm, 470 nm, and 561 nm laser excitations and the overlay channel, respectively. (B) CLSM images of m-CCD treated E. coli with time-lapse to observe the fluorescence decay at different time intervals from zeroth to 120 min (zoomed image is available in Fig. S8). (C) CLSM images of a polymicrobial solution consisting of E. coli and S. aureus cells incubated with m-CCD (zoomed image is available in Fig. S9) (D) Z-stack confocal imaging of m-CCD treated E. coli cells indicating internalization of m-CCD into Gram-negative bacterial cells.
Illustration of the microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of m-CCD having surface passivated colistin moieties with multi-emissive properties.
FLT data including lifetime components and amplitude fractions of free m-CCD & m-CCD treated E. coli cells at five data points.
Fluorometric sensor performance in different types of samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: x = CFU × 106 mL−1, L1 = Linear range for lower bacterial cell concentrations, L2 = Linear Range for higher cell concentrations.

head.ce:title: Revolutionizing Gram-negative bacteria detection: FLIM and multicolor imaging based selective interaction study using colistin passivated carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rising prevalence of antibiotic-resistant Gram-negative bacteria presents a major challenge for healthcare and requires the development of new and effective detection techniques. In this study, we present a novel and simple method for the selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria using multi-emissive colistin-passivated carbon dots (m-CCD) based on Fluorescence Lifetime (FLT) response and bioimaging. The m-CCD was synthesized using a one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal process, and its interaction with Gram-negative bacteria was studied using Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) and automated Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM). The investigations revealed a notable reduction in the FLT of m-CCD-treated Gram-negative bacteria (average lifetime of m-CCD, (τ avg ) m-CCD is 3.91 ns and m-CCD treated Gram-negative bacteria, (τ avg ) m-CCD-bac is 1.57 ns), demonstrating the selective interaction between them. Furthermore, the CLSM data revealed high specificity of m-CCD for Gram-negative bacteria, even in polymicrobial samples. Importantly, we also demonstrate that this method can quantify Escherichia coli (E. coli) cells with two linear ranges such as 3.40 × 105 – 9.80 × 105 CFU mL−1 and 6.90 × 107–4.14 × 108 CFU mL−1 in standard culture and real samples such as tap water and human urine with a limit of detection ∼ 3.68–4.89 × 104 CFU mL−1. This straightforward method has great potential for the rapid and selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria, paving the way for early diagnosis and treatment of bacterial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Outbreaks of infectious diseases caused by pathogenic bacteria pose a serious threat to the survival of mankind, with a huge burden on global healthcare infrastructure. Bacteria-borne infectious diseases have a significant mortality rate and could severely affect the economic progress of the world and social stability [1]. Traditional bacterial diagnostic methods such as culturing and microscopic analysis [2], Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) [3] and immunoassays [4] have been used extensively. However, the potential drawbacks associated with these techniques cannot be overlooked. This includes time-consuming assays, low accuracy, poor sensitivity and selectivity, false-positive results, laborious sample and reagent preparation, and manual intervention [5]. In recent times, plethora of alternate strategies have been designed to overcome these limitations like quartz crystal microbalance [6,7], electrochemical [8] and optical based bacteria detection [9,10] methods. Of late, fluorescence nanoparticles (FNP) have emerged as an effective option for bacteria sensing and bioimaging with higher precision, sensitivity and selectivity [11,12].

Carbon dots are an emerging class of FNPs with a typical size range of 1–10 nm [13]. It consists of a carbon core, and its surface properties can be modulated by functionalizing it with various groups [14]. These are widely used for different applications due to their intriguing properties like photostability, biocompatibility, resistance to photobleaching, non-toxicity to live cells, efficient drug delivery, remarkable photocatalytic activity, and many more [15,16]. The applications of carbon dots have been showcased in bioimaging [17], heavy metal sensing [18], myoglobin [19], protein [20,21] and nucleic acid [22] estimation. Recently, carbon dots have been deployed for coherent identification of pathogenic bacteria [23]. In a similar context, different modifications such as doping, pH change, and precursors variations used to synthesize carbon dots and have been utilized in detection of pathogenic bacteria [24–26]. The specific identification of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria has been attained by synthesizing uniquely modified carbon dots [23]. For example, positively charged carbon dots were synthesized using quaternary ammonium compounds for the specific detection of Gram-positive bacteria [27,28]. Similarly, multiple approaches have been adopted to synthesize carbon dots specific to Gram-negative bacteria as well [29,30].

Antibiotics are used against various strains of Gram-negative bacteria such as E. coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), and Acinetobacter, resulting in the killing of bacterial cells [31,32]. Colistin ( Polymixin E), a cationic polypeptide antibiotic, has a higher affinity towards lipopolysaccharides (LPS) and phospholipids in the outer membrane of the Gram-negative bacterial cell wall. This results in cell wall disruption and leakage of cytoplasmic content during cell lysis [33]. The quantum dots synthesized using CdSe/ZnS and functionalized with colistin have been deployed to detect E. coli cells [34]. Specific detection of E. coli was demonstrated using carbon dots derived from antibiotics like amikacin [35] and colistin [36]. Despite the efforts to realize antibiotic functionalized carbon dots as an effective option for selective detection of pathogenic bacteria, a detailed study on their interaction is still a big void.

Therefore, in this work, we present a comprehensive explanation on the highly selective interaction of m-CCD towards Gram-negative bacteria based on experimental evidence obtained from FLIM measurements, quantification assays and bio-imaging analysis. A few important FLT studies have been carried out to understand the optical properties of carbon dots during pH change [37], inner filter effect, Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) [38] and fluorescence quenching [39,40]. However, in this study, FLT measurements of free m-CCD and m-CCD treated E. coli cells were analyzed to explain the interaction of m-CCD with E. coli cells. The quantification of m-CCD treated E. coli cells was screened in standard and real samples such as tap water and human urine with decent detection range. The obtained results were corroborated with the CLSM based bio-imaging analysis in standard and polymicrobial samples. To the best of our knowledge, single molecule FLIM based technique has never been explored previously to study the specific interaction between the colistin passivated carbon dots and Gram-negative bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of colistin derived fluorescent m-CCD
ce:section.ce:para: The microwave-assisted synthesis pathway of m-CCD has been illustrated in Schematic 1. m-CCD was synthesized by mixing 1 g of citric acid, 500 μL of ethylenediamine (EDA) and 15 mg colistin thoroughly in a conical flask and the solution volume was made up to 10 mL using autoclaved Milli-Q water. The carbon dots synthesis was performed in a domestic microwave oven for 15 min. The charred mass in the conical flask was cooled to room temperature and dissolved in 10 mL ultra-pure MilliQ water followed by ultrasonication for 10 min to dissolve the large precipitates. The m-CCD suspension was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 60 min and then purified using a 0.22-micron syringe filter, followed by overnight dialysis using a 1 kDa dialysis membrane. m-CCD stock solution (4.38 mg mL−1) was diluted to 0.1 mg mL−1 using Milli-Q water for characterization and bacterial interaction studies. The detailed methodologies adapted for synthesis, characterization, bacterial cell culture and serial dilution protocol have been explained in the supplementary material (S. 1 to S. 7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation of m-CCD treated bacteria for spectroscopic and microscopic analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To study the fluorescence emission response of m-CCD treated bacterial cells, different bacterial strains such as E. coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) were used. The sterile LB media was used to grow the cells at 37 °C and 180 rpm in a shaker incubator for 10 h (OD600 ∼ 1). The cells were washed with Milli-Q water to remove the media as supernatant by centrifuging at 5500 rpm for 10 min. The detailed protocol of bacterial growth and serial dilutions for quantitative analysis have been discussed in the supplementary material (S. 7). Finally, the bacterial cells were incubated with m-CCD solution (0.1 mg mL−1) at 37 °C for 1 h. Post incubation, the cells were washed twice with autoclaved Milli-Q water to remove the unbound m-CCD particles. The spectral properties of m-CCD treated bacterial cells were measured at λex/λem ∼ 350/450 nm.

ce:list-item.ce:label: a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: The selective labelling of m-CCD towards Gram-negative bacteria was studied by CLSM imaging. CLSM imaging was carried out after optimizing various parameters such as magnification, focus adjustment, laser power and detector gain. The samples were prepared on a glass slide fixed with a cover slip having mounting media. The slides were placed on top of the objective upside down of the inverted microscope. The background was checked to verify the absence of autofluorescence by using controls, and the laser power was adjusted to avoid any interference. The images of m-CCD treated bacteria were acquired in a sequential mode with lasers of different wavelengths such as 405 nm, 470 nm, and 561 nm.

ce:para.ce:bold: Fluorescence Lifetime (FLT) measurements of m-CCD treated bacteria using FLIM

ce:section.ce:para: FLIM imaging of m-CCD treated E. coli was conducted using the MicroTime 200 time-resolved confocal microscope (PicoQuant) with 60 × 1.2 NA water immersion objective. The sample was excited with 405 nm, pulsed diode lasers at 40 MHz repetition rate having a pulse width of 52 ps. An appropriate dichroic mirror (405/488 nm) facilitated to separate fluorescence from the excitation line. The emission was observed from 425 nm onwards (using a long pass filter of 425 nm). A 50 µm pinhole was used to direct the fluorescence onto a single photon counting ȃvalanche photodiode detector to capture the fluorescence autocorrelation traces. Each decay curve was tri-exponentially fitted using a deconvolution approach with the help of SymPhoTime 64 software wherein the Full Width Half Maxima (FWHM) of Instrument Response Function (IRF) is 450 ps.

FLIM experiments were carried out via point measurement after selecting an appropriate scanned area of interest with m-CCD treated E. coli cells. The measurements were taken from different points of the bacterial cell wall and cytoplasm, with acquisition times of 30 s each. Data collected with a good average count signal (30–40 kcps) at a laser power of 80 µW. The acquisition of freshly prepared samples was carried out in triplicates at different Region of Interest (ROI 1, 2 & 3), keeping all measurement conditions fixed. FLT decay for m-CCD treated E. coli cells was measured at five different points located on cell wall (Points 1, 2, 3 & 4) and cytoplasm (Point 5). FLT decay curve with three exponential components of amplitude (A1, A2 & A3) and lifetime (τ1, τ2 & τ3) with decent χ2 values (∼ 1) demonstrate a good fit. The tabulated data for three components of amplitude and lifetime of free m-CCD and m-CCD treated E. coli (in triplicates, ROI 1–3) has been shown in Table S1. FLT decay fit indicates m-CCD has been distributed all over the bacterial cells. The minor variations in FLT values at different points in each bacterial cell are justified by the given error limits.

HR-TEM micrograph of m-CCD exhibits mono-dispersed and uniform spherical particles, as shown in Fig. 1 A. The average diameter of the synthesized particle was 4.6 ± 0.9 nm as judged from forty-five individual particles. The lattice plane of m-CCD is complementary to the plane of graphitic carbon (1 0 0) and is commonly assigned to turbostratic disorder in multiple stacked sheets. The broad diffraction peak at 23.10° (Fig. 1 B) in the XRD pattern of the synthesized m-CCD corresponds to the amorphous characteristics of graphitic carbon.

The average hydrodynamic diameter of m-CCD was estimated to be 3.45 nm by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) (Fig. S1) and was found to be in close agreement with HR-TEM analysis, as shown in section S. 4.

The spectroscopic properties of m-CCD were studied using Ultraviolet-Visible (UV-Vis) absorption and Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy. The UV-Vis absorption plot of m-CCD typically exhibits two ultraviolet peaks with a tail stretching to the visible region (Fig. 1 C). The small shoulder peak at 240 nm is assigned to π–π * transition of sp2 carbonized domain. A relatively stronger peak at 350 nm corresponds to the n–π * transition due to the bulk availability of nitrogen-containing functional groups and carbonyl groups having lone pairs of electrons. The m-CCD was water dispersible due to the presence of several polar functionalities such as hydroxyl (O-H), amine (N-H) and carboxylic (-COOH) groups. The stock solution appeared to be dark brown colored, but the diluted solution was pale yellow in daylight. The m-CCD exhibited stable, bright blue fluorescence under UV lamp, as showed in the inset of Fig. 1 C.

Photo Luminescent Quantum Yield (PLQY) was calculated to be 25.1 % (equation 1, S. 5), and the explanation is provided in S. 5. The highest PL emission peak (λem) of m-CCD was observed at 450 nm, corresponding to 350 nm excitation wavelength (λex) (Fig. 1 C). Furthermore, the PL emission was scanned at different excitation wavelengths, from 300 nm to 500 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 D.

The emission scan exhibited a remarkable increase in fluorescence intensity from 300 nm to 370 nm and subsequently relinquished from 380 nm onwards with negligible or no redshift. This response is due to the surface state defects caused by the controlled reaction environment. Moreover, the uniform particle size of the m-CCD contributes to the reasoning of negligible redshift. The different size particles were not available to induce the formation of significant transition states within the bandgap, and hence the insignificant redshift was only observed at longer excitation wavelengths [41]. It could also be due to the availability of similar surface functional groups due to uniform surface oxidation and amidation of nitrogen dopants provided by EDA and colistin moiety [42].

Further, the scan showed reduced fluorescence intensity at 430 nm excitation; however, the redshift is prominently evident from 430 nm to 500 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 D (inset). This trend can be explained based on the activation of energy traps at lower excitation wavelengths, whereas only one of the emission centers remained active at longer wavelengths (450 nm). [43].

FLT is an intrinsic characteristic of fluorescent nanomaterials and is usually independent of signal amplitude and is a function of factors such as reporter concentration. However, in previous reports, FLT of CDs depends on excitation or emission wavelength, which could be attributed to the effect of surface state [37,44]. In this work, our approach is to explore the FLT-based study for a better understanding of m-CCD interaction with Gram-negative bacteria. The characteristic change in FLT of m-CCD in the absence and presence of bacterial cells reveals the interaction of the synthesized CD with the Gram-negative bacteria. FLT decay of free m-CCD (as shown in Fig. 1 E) was measured at 320 nm excitation wavelength and estimated at 430 nm emission wavelength. The average lifetime (τ avg ) m-CCD was calculated to be 3.91 ns with three individual lifetime components τ1 (13.44 ns), τ2 (0.91 ns) & τ3 (4.53 ns) and amplitude fractions A1 (0.13), A2 (0.49) and A3 (0.38), respectively. These components most likely exist due to different molecular orientations, conformations, or interactions of m-CCD molecules within the solution phase. The longer FLT component (τ1 =13.44 ns) makes m-CCD a fluorescent probe with superior optical and electronic properties. Nevertheless, a more detailed study of the decay dynamics is necessary for a better understanding.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analysis was carried out on the precursors (like colistin) and the synthesized m-CCD to confirm the surface passivation of colistin moiety. The IR peak at 3180 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H groups due to intramolecular hydrogen bonding (Fig. 1 F). The small IR absorption peak at 2900 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of C-H groups. The slight shift observed in the stretching vibration of CO at 1650 cm−1 of m-CCD from 1642 cm−1 of colistin precursor stipulates the formation of an amide-type bond. This corroborates the incorporation of colistin into the surface of synthesized m-CCD. Similarly, the strong peak at 1610 cm−1 of m-CCD indicates the presence of primary amine derived from the colistin precursor. The peak at 1350 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C-N bond. The information revealed from the FTIR study has been reaffirmed with X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis data to strongly validate the passivation of colistin residue over the surface of m-CCD.

The wide scan XPS spectrum of m-CCD shows the presence of carbon (C 1 s), nitrogen (N 1 s) and oxygen (O 1 s) at 284.5, 401.0, and 532.5 eV binding energies (Fig. S2). The detailed elemental composition of m-CCD is presented in section S. 6. The XPS data was found to be in close agreement with the FTIR data and confirms the passivation of colistin on the surface of m-CCD.

PL emission of m-CCD was tested in various chemical conditions such as pH, ionic concentration, and temperature, and presented in section S. 8 (Fig. S3). The reaction parameters, such as reaction time and weight of colistin precursor, were also optimized as a function of PL emission intensity and are discussed in section S. 9 (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FLIM based FLT measurement of m-CCD treated bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Depending upon the fluorescence of the m-CCD and surrounding environment, time-resolved fluorescence properties may vary from ps to hundreds of ns. Therefore, FLIM based FLT measurement of m-CCD treated bacteria was compared to analyze the variations in the FLT signals for free m-CCD as shown in Fig. 2. FLT measurements of m-CCD treated bacteria were carried out on the outer membrane of bacterial cells and in the cytoplasmic region of bacteria to realize the change in FLT. Modulations in the structural conformation of CDs facilitate their significance when binding with bacterial cells. It was observed that bacterial cells emit fluorescence signals when it binds with m-CCD. The specific binding mechanism of m-CCD with Gram-negative bacteria such as E. coli is based on the activity of surface passivated polycationic colistin moiety. The well-known mechanism of colistin action against Gram-negative bacteria such as E. coli allows the specific and selective interaction of m-CCD. The polycationic nature of colistin helps to displace divalent cations such as Mg++ and Ca++ ions available to integrate the lipopolysaccharide (LPS) in the outer membrane once m-CCD reaches close vicinity of Gram-negative bacteria. This displacement of divalent cations results in the generation of membrane pores. The outer membrane of the cell wall loses its permeability, due to which the colistin capped fluorescent carbon dots enter the Gram-negative bacterial cell [31,33].

According to our approach, we measured FLT decay curves of the E. coli cells at a single molecule level. Since the entire bacterial cell was fluorescing, five points (cell wall (1, 2, 3 & 4) and cytoplasmic region (5)) were selected to confirm the difference between their lifetimes.

FLT decay of m-CCD treated E. coli cells (as shown in Fig. 2) was measured at 405 nm excitation wavelength and estimated at 425 nm (Long Pass filter) emission wavelength. The average lifetime (τ avg ) m-CCD-bac calculated at different points varies from 1.51 ns to 1.64 ns comprising three individual lifetime components τ1 (6.07–6.4 ns), τ2 (0.56–0.6 ns) & τ3 (2.07–2.3 ns) and amplitude fractions A1 (0.09–0.1), A2 (0.63–0.65) and A3 (0.26–0.27), respectively, as shown in Table 1.

A significant reduction of an average lifetime was observed (from 3.91 ns to 1.57 ns) for free m-CCD as compared to m-CCD treated E. coli. The details of average lifetime calculations for free m-CCD and m-CCD treated E. coli have been provided in the section S.10.

Perhaps, the interaction of m-CCD with Gram-negative bacteria resulted in a comprehensive participation of m-CCD in the non-radiative process equally in all three components due to which the significant reduction in the FLT was observed [45]. Moreover, there is no remarkable shift in FLT profile while changing location from the outer membrane to the cytoplasmic region of the bacterial cell. The FLT data of (τ avg ) m-CCD-bac was authenticated by repeating the FLT measurements at three ROI as a triplicate data at five different data points (Section 2.2 b, Fig. 2 B-D). The measurement exhibited an almost similar average lifetime among all the selected ROI and lies in the range of (τ avg ) m-CCD-bac ∼ 1.34–1.68 ns (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of m-CCD treated E. coli cells

ce:list-item.ce:label: a.
ce:list-item.ce:para: To demonstrate the quantification assay in standard sample, different concentrations of E. coli cells were prepared and treated with m-CCD (0.1 mg mL−1) in triplicates. PL emission responses of m-CCD treated E. coli cells with varying concentrations were investigated at λex ∼ 350 nm and depicted in Fig. 3 A. The two linear regression equations ( Table 2) of m-CCD treated E. coli cells in standard culture were y = 11.03 x + 77.36 (R1 2 = 0.998, 3.40 × 105 – 9.80 × 105 CFU mL−1) and y = 1.46 x + 92.95 (R2 2 = 0.990, 6.90 × 107 – 4.14 × 108 CFU mL−1) and LOD was 3.76 × 104 CFU mL−1. The linear ranges obtained using linear regression equations as shown in Fig. 3 A and presented in Table 2. The calculation of LOD for the standard sample has been discussed in S. 10. The broad detection range (ƩL) 3.40 × 105 – 4.14 × 108 CFU mL−1 (Cumulative, ƩR2 = 0.974) has also been manifested in Fig. S5 (inset) and Table S2. These results indicated that the simultaneous sensing of m-CCD treated E. coli cells is feasible and consistent.

ce:section.ce:para:
The real samples, such as tap water and human urine, were collected in a sterile container, and spiked with E. coli cells. The samples were prepared in triplicates and treated with m-CCD (0.1 mg mL−1). PL emission responses of m-CCD treated E. coli cells in tap water and urine sample were analyzed at λex ∼ 350 nm and shown in Fig. 3 B and C. The gradual reduction in the PL emission intensity was observed clearly from 108 to 105 CFU mL−1 of m-CCD treated E. coli cells present in the real samples. The two linear regression equations (Table 2) of m-CCD treated E. coli cells present in tap water were y = 8.49 x + 65.41 (R1 2 = 0.980, 3.40 × 105 – 9.80 × 105 CFU mL−1) and y = 1.42 x + 63.39 (R2 2 = 0.942, 6.90 × 107 – 4.14 × 108 CFU mL−1) and LOD was 4.89 × 104 CFU mL−1 as shown in Fig. 3 B. Similarly, for urine samples, two linear regression equations of m-CCD treated E. coli cells were y = 11.28 x + 64.04 (R1 2 = 0.934, 3.40 × 105 – 9.80 × 105 CFU mL−1) and y = 1.30 x + 112.20 (R2 2 = 0.992, 6.90 × 107 – 4.14 × 108 CFU mL−1) and LOD was 3.68 × 104 CFU mL−1, as presented in Fig. 3 C and Table 2. The broad detection ranges (ƩL) 3.40 × 105 – 4.14 × 108 CFU mL−1 (Cumulative, ƩR2 = 0.970 and 0.965) for both the real samples have also been displayed in Fig. S5 and Table S2. These studies demonstrate the potential of m-CCD as a promising tool for detecting Gram-negative bacteria in samples of different matrices. The calculation of LOD for the real samples such as tap water and urine has been shown in S. 11. These results demonstrate the simultaneous sensing of m-CCD treated E. coli cells is feasible and consistent in real samples.
a.
Statistical method to establish significant variance in the fluorescence response of m-CCD treated Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is an unsupervised statistical method that aims to maximize the variance ratio between different classes and within each class. This enables the distinction of response patterns within the data. Here, m-CCD treated two different classes of bacteria viz, Gram-positive, and Gram-negative have been used to evaluate the significant variance in the fluorescence response as shown in Fig. 3 D. The sensor array was designed for the triplicates of each m-CCD treated bacteria generating a matrix of 4 m-CCD treated bacteria × triplicates × 4 cell concentrations (108 to 105 CFU mL−1). As demonstrated in Fig. 3 D, the m-CCD treated Gram-negative bacteria are the main contributors to PC1 (79.79 %) as E. coli and P. aeruginosa lies towards longer axis of PC1. On the other hand, the PCA score plot shows m-CCD treated S. aureus and B. subtilis on the shorter axis with clear clustering and no significant overlap. This clearly demonstrates the significant variation between the m-CCD treated Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacteria with no visible overlaps and hence justify the selectivity of m-CCD towards Gram-negative bacteria.

Table T S. 3 depicted the comparative analysis of the linear ranges and LOD obtained from m-CCD treated E. coli cells with the reported CDs for pathogenic bacteria detection to give a broader picture on the significant bacteria sensing abilities of m-CCD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of m-CCD towards E. coli over S. aureus cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 A and C shows the PL emission spectra of m-CCD (0.1 mg mL−1) treated E. coli and S. aureus cells at OD values 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, and 1.0, respectively. The variations in PL emission intensities were studied at λex/ λem ∼ 350/450 nm for the bacterial cell concentration at different OD600 (Fig. 4 B and D). The PL emission response of m-CCD treated bacterial cells at OD 0.1–1.0 showed an elevated trend for E. coli cells as compared to S. aureus. The significant change in PL emission response of m-CCD treated Gram-negative bacteria at different OD demonstrates its unique selectivity. Furthermore, the selective PL emission response was also supported by analysing the fluorescence spectra of m-CCD treated E. coli and S. aureus separately as shown in Fig. 4 E. The feeble PL emission intensity in m-CCD treated S. aureus describes the poor interaction between the m-CCD and the cell wall of S. aureus bacteria due to the absence of LPS membrane.

The sample preparation of m-CCD treated bacteria with control have been discussed in S. 12. The morphological analysis to represent the mechanism of m-CCD action with the LPS of Gram-negative bacteria cell wall has been carried out using TEM (Fig. S6) and explained in S. 13.

PL emission response of m-CCD treated different bacterial cells such as E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and B. subtilis were measured to test the ability of m-CCD in distinguishing Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria selectively. The specific controls were also tested to cancel out the possibility of interference due to autofluorescence in the spectroscopic analysis. Fig. 4 F exhibits the PL emission response of m-CCD treated bacterial cells at λex/ λem ∼ 350/ 450 nm. PL emission from m-CCD treated E. coli and P. aeruginosa was much higher than S. aureus, and B. subtilis and therefore confirms the specific nature of m-CCD towards Gram-negative bacteria.

The brilliant multi-emissive fluorescence properties of m-CCD was utilized for imaging and differentiating Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. The selective labelling of E. coli and P. aeruginosa using m-CCD has been shown in Fig. 5 A. The Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial cells were incubated with 0.1 mg mL−1 of m-CCD for 1 h, and after washing twice, the bacterial cells were imaged using CLSM with 405 nm, 470 nm, and 561 nm lasers. It was clearly observed that m-CCD treated Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli and P. aeruginosa) manifested multi-color emissions, however for Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and B. subtilis) the fluorescent signals were not observed. The untreated Gram-negative (E. coli and P. aeruginosa) and Gram-positive (S. aureus and B. subtilis) bacteria were used as controls to verify the absence of autofluorescence and shown in Fig. S7.

A time-lapse study was performed on m-CCD treated E. coli cells to investigate the fluorescence sustainability over an extended period (Fig. 5 B). It was found that the fluorescence remained stable for most of the 120 min irradiation period (using 561 nm laser). However, the fluorescence of m-CCD treated E. coli cells started to degenerate after 60 min and slowly deteriorate till 120 min. The expanded CLSM images of m-CCD treated E. coli cells at different time point have been shown in Fig. S8.

E. coli and S. aureus cells were distinguished using m-CCD in polymicrobial samples using laser of different wavelengths, such as 405 nm, 470 nm, and 561 nm, as shown in Fig. 5 C. The m-CCD treated E. coli bacterial cells under different excitation wavelengths exhibited significant blue, green and red color emissions. On the other hand, S. aureus did not exhibit any noticeable fluorescence. The overlay images display the m-CCD treated E. coli cells (pointers in Fig. 5 C). The enlarged CLSM images of m-CCD treated E. coli and S. aureus in a polymicrobial sample is shown in Fig. S9.

Z-stacking confocal imaging of m-CCD treated E. coli cells was carried out at 405 nm, 470 nm, and 561 nm excitation lasers, as shown in Fig. 5 D. The purpose of Z-stacking is to confirm the cell internalization or endocytosis of m-CCD inside the bacterial cells, which occurs during the interaction of divalent cations of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) present on the outer membrane with polycationic colistin moiety passivated on the surface of m-CCD. Z-stacking exhibited the presence of colocalization observed after merging the images of m-CCD treated E. coli obtained using 405 nm, 470 nm and 561 nm excitation. The presence of a yellow region (Fig. 5 D) in the merged image of m-CCD treated bacterial cell (visible between the green and red regions) could be due to colocalization ascribed to spatial overlap of multicolor m-CCD.
This can be studied more intuitively to draw some interesting outcomes, especially on biomolecular interactions with the help of multi-emissive carbon dots.

In conclusion, this work demonstrates a highly efficient one-step microwave-assisted synthesis of colistin carbon dots (m-CCD) as a fluorescent probe for the specific detection of Gram-negative bacteria. The stability of the synthesized carbon dots was analysed and optimized at different parameters, such as ionic concentrations, temperature, and pH. FLIM analysis exhibited the reduction in fluorescence lifetime of m-CCD when interacted with Gram-negative bacteria. The efficiency of m-CCD as a fluorescent probe was assessed by quantifying Gram-negative bacteria in standard and real samples such as tap water and human urine. The results showed a broad detection range and good linearity. PCA results showed the unique specificity of m-CCD towards Gram-negative bacteria. CLSM imaging of m-CCD treated Gram-negative bacteria showed the efficient bioimaging abilities of the synthesized carbon dots. Moreover, the selective nature of m-CCD was demonstrated by identifying the Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) in a polymicrobial sample. In summary, the results presented in the paper on m-CCD based detection of Gram-negative bacteria offer a wider perspective on improving diagnostics. The utilization of m-CCD brings forth enhanced sensitivity, simplicity, and portability to bacterial detection methods. These advancements have the potential to revolutionize diagnostics, leading to rapid and accurate identification of bacterial infections, improved patient care, and the development of efficient point-of-care testing devices.

Abhishek Pathak: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Punnakkal Navaneeth: Methodology, Visualization. Monika Gupta: Investigation, Methodology. Aarathi Pradeep: Validation and Proof-reading. Bipin G. Nair: Funding acquisition, Resources. Punathil Vasu Suneesh: Validation, Formal analysis, Project administration. Ravikrishnan Elangovan: Formal analysis, Resources, Validation. Lotta-Riina Sundberg: Formal analysis, Resources, Validation. Varpu Marjomäki: Formal analysis, Resources, Validation, Proof-reading. T.G. Satheesh Babu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: T. G. Satheesh Babu reports was provided by Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham. T. G. Satheesh Babu reports financial support was provided by India Ministry of Science & Technology Department of Biotechnology. T. G. Satheesh Babu reports a relationship with Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham that includes: employment. We wish to confirm that there are no known conflicts of interest associated with this publication and there has been no significant financial support for this work that could have influenced its outcome. The author(s) declares no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. The authors thank Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), CUSAT, Kerala, for their generous help in HR-TEM imaging of m-CCD. The authors also thank the Indian Institute of Technology-Kanpur for performing the XPS characterization of m-CCD. The authors are grateful to Sophisticated Analytical & Technical Help Institutes (SATHI) facilities situated at IIT-Delhi Sonipat campus for allowing us to carry out the advanced confocal and fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopic analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134433.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Understanding and minimizing non-radiative recombination losses in perovskite light emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Cheng, Y. Feng, Y. Fu, Y. Zheng, Y. Shao, Y. Bai, Understanding and minimizing non-radiative recombination losses in perovskite light emitting diodes, J. Mater. Chem. C, 10 (2022), 13590–13610.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Abhishek Pathak has received his Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering (2021) from Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Coimbatore. Currently, he is working as a post-doctoral researcher at University of Jyväskylä, Finland. As a graduate researcher, Abhishek developed optical and electrochemical sensors based on zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials. These biosensors were used for sensitive and selective detection of pathogenic bacteria. His research interests include biosensors, microbiology, cancer biology, and advanced fluorescence microscopy.

Punnakkal Navaneeth completed integrated M.Sc. chemistry from Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Coimbatore. Currently perusing Ph.D. from Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham Coimbatore. His research interests include flexible energy storage devices and development of non-enzymatic bio sensors.

Monika Gupta was born in Allahabad city of Uttar Pradesh of India. She Graduated from Allahabad University with bachelor’s degree in chemistry, Botany in 2006 then Masters degree in organic Chemistry in 2008. Later she lived in Mumbai to complete her Doctorate degree from Bhabha Atomic Research Centre. She was awarded with CAS PIFI fellowship in China and DST SERB NPDF fellowship in India in 2016 and worked as a Post Doctorate fellow with Prof. Chuanlang Zhan for two years at ICCAS in Beijing till 2018. She joined Indian Institute of Technology Delhi as a Post Doctorate in 2019 under Professor Pramit K Chowdhury. Her research area includes synthesis of organic small molecules for Solar cells, fluorescent dye molecules, host-guest complexation and photochemistry, protein dynamics, folding-unfolding, biophysics.

Aarathi Pradeep received her Ph.D. from Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Coimbatore (2020). She is presently working as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Sciences, Amrita School of Physical Sciences, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham. Her research interests include engineering microfluidics for point-of-care diagnostic devices.

Bipin G. Nair Professor and Dean, Life Sciences and School of Biotechnology, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Amritapuri. He obtained his master's and doctoral degrees from Maharaja Sayajirao University in 1981 and 1986, respectively. He received Postdoctoral research training from the University of Tennessee, Memphis. His research interests include antimicrobial resistance, the mechanism underlying impaired wound healing and the development of low-cost biomedical devices.

Punathil Vasu Suneesh received his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry from Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Coimbatore (2015). He is presently working as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Sciences, Amrita School of Physical Sciences, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham. His research interests are in the fabrication of Point-of-Care Diagnostic Devices and Electrochemical Energy Storage Devices.

Ravikrishnan Elangovan is a faculty in Department of Biochemical Engg and Biotechnology, IIT Delhi since 2010. He is an undergraduate from IIT-Delhi and Ph.D. from University of Florence, Italy. Ravi is a biophysicist by training, is actively engaged in developing methods/technologies for visualizing biological systems. Ravi and his team has developed multiple platform technologies for infectious disease diagnosis. Ravi is Indian coordinator for DOSA - Diagnostics for One Health and User Driven Solutions for AMR project.

Lotta-Riina Sundberg Professor in Cell and Molecular Biology in University of Jyväskylä, at the Department of Biological and Environmental Science, and the Scientific Director of Nanoscience Center. Sundberg is interested in various aspects of bacterial pathogens and their prevention, phage-bacterium interactions, phage therapy and environmental microbiology.

Varpu Majomäki Professor in Cell and Molecular biology, and Vice-Head of the Department of Biological and Environmental science/University of Jyväskylä, is an expert in enterovirus and coronavirus cell biology, experience in mentoring students (19 completed, 3 on-going) and leading projects granted by Academy of Finland (5), Business Finland (4), and Jane and Aatos Erkko foundation (2) for studying infectious entry of viruses, developing anti-virals and elucidating the mechanistical basis of their action. She has 118 publications, h-index of 43, i10-index of 83, and 5473 citations (Google scholar). She has developed software for biological imaging and has two patents on antiviral molecules. She actively collaborates with Finnish and international research groups and has ongoing projects with Finnish companies for developing antiviral solutions.

T.G. Satheesh Babu received his Ph.D. from Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Coimbatore. Currently, he is a Professor in the Department of Sciences, Amrita School of Physical Sciences and heading the Biomedical Engineering Centre at Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Coimbatore. His research focused on developing affordable point-of-care diagnostic devices, including optical and electrochemical biosensors, Lab-on-a-Chip devices, and paper diagnostic devices.
